local_names.hindi: टोंकोत्सु रामेन (सूअर की हड्डी का सूप)

aliases: 猪骨拉面 (Zhū Gǔ Lā Miàn)
टोंकोत्सु रामेन (सूअर की हड्डी का सूप)

chinese: 猪骨拉面 (Zhū Gǔ Lā Miàn)
hindi: टोंकोत्सु रामेन (सूअर की हड्डी का सूप)

region_of_origin: Fukuoka (Hakata), Kyushu, Japan; served in Singapore restaurant context
food_subgroup: Noodle soup — pork bone broth ramen with wheat noodles, chashu pork, marinated egg, nori, bamboo shoots, black garlic oil

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Tonkotsu Ramen as a prepared restaurant composite dish. Closest parent would be a noodle soup food product; no exact URI assigned.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle soup, pork-based broth, with toppings (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0JKG used as closest parent for pork-broth noodle soup composite. No exact Tonkotsu Ramen code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Tonkotsu Ramen as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving is approximately 600g comprising pork bone broth (~300ml), cooked wheat ramen noodles (~150g), and toppings including chashu pork, marinated egg, nori, bamboo shoots, spring onion, and black garlic oil (~150g combined).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodle soup with pork, composite estimate; cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database for sodium and broth contribution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Tonkotsu Ramen as a complete restaurant dish. Values derived by component calculation: cooked wheat noodles (USDA FDC #20121), pork belly braised (USDA FDC #167903), hard-boiled egg (USDA FDC #173424), nori (USDA FDC #168457), bamboo shoots (USDA FDC #11026), and pork bone broth estimated from HPB Singapore broth data. Sodium value (~333mg/100g, ~2000mg/600g bowl) cross-validated against HPB Singapore restaurant noodle soup data. Per-100g values represent the blended composite of the full 600g bowl.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of wheat noodles in fatty broth — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for tonkotsu ramen
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for Tonkotsu Ramen exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~55 estimated from the closest analogous entry: wheat noodles in fatty broth (GI ~50–58 range). The significant fat content from pork bone broth and chashu pork moderates the GI of plain wheat noodles (GI ~58–65) downward to approximately 55. GL per 600g serving calculated as: (55 × 72g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 39.6. Available carbohydrate per 600g bowl = 12.0 g/100g × 6 = 72g.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content (~2,000mg per 600g bowl = ~100% of WHO 2,000mg daily limit in a single meal)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit tonkotsu ramen; if consumed, leaving at least half the broth unconsumed reduces sodium intake by approximately 500–600mg, but the dish remains a high-sodium risk even with partial broth consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~40 per bowl) from 72g available carbohydrate in wheat ramen noodles, significantly exceeding the recommended <20 GL per meal for T2DM management
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat tonkotsu ramen as an occasional meal only; the 72g carbohydrate load per bowl is equivalent to approximately 1.5 cups of cooked rice and requires careful insulin or medication adjustment, and portion reduction (e.g., half-portion noodles) is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined high sodium (~2,000mg/bowl), saturated fat (~12g/bowl from pork belly and bone broth), and cholesterol (~180mg/bowl) present compounded cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit tonkotsu ramen to rare occasions; the combination of high sodium, saturated fat from chashu pork belly, and cholesterol from egg and pork exceeds recommended daily limits for multiple cardiovascular risk factors simultaneously.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High chronic sodium intake may contribute to systemic inflammation; saturated fat from chashu pork belly is moderate at ~12g per bowl
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tonkotsu ramen is not specifically pro-inflammatory but the high sodium content warrants limitation for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis managing inflammation through dietary means.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tonkotsu Ramen (Singapore Restaurant Style) is a Japanese pork bone broth noodle soup served as a complete restaurant dish in Singapore ramen chains such as Ippudo, Ramen Keisuke, Ichiran, and Menya Musashi. A standard bowl (~600g) comprises approximately 150g of cooked thin wheat ramen noodles, 300ml of rich milky-white tonkotsu broth made by boiling pork bones for 8–12 hours until collagen emulsifies into a creamy opaque soup, 50g of chashu braised pork belly slices, one soft-boiled soy-marinated egg (ajitsuke tamago), two sheets of nori (dried seaweed), 20g of bamboo shoots (menma), spring onion, and a drizzle of black garlic oil (mayu). The broth is intensely savoury, rich, and fatty with umami depth from the pork collagen. Per 100g the dish provides approximately 125 kcal, 5.3g protein, 5.2g fat, and 12g carbohydrate. A full 600g bowl delivers approximately 750 kcal, 32g protein, 31g fat, 72g carbohydrate, and critically ~2,000mg sodium — equivalent to 100% of the WHO daily sodium recommendation in a single meal. GI is approximately 55 (low category) due to fat moderating carbohydrate absorption, but the glycemic load per bowl is ~40, which is high for a single meal. The dish is NOT halal (contains pork bone broth and chashu pork belly). It is dairy-free. NOVA Class 3 (processed). Key clinical concerns: highest single-dish sodium risk among common Singapore restaurant meals; high GL for T2DM management; combined saturated fat and sodium risk for cardiovascular disease. Halving broth consumption reduces sodium by approximately 500–600mg.
common_user_queries: how much sodium in ramen singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central entries for individual ingredients (wheat noodles, pork belly, egg, nori, bamboo shoots) and HPB Singapore broth data cross-reference. No single approved-source entry exists for Tonkotsu Ramen as a complete restaurant dish. Sodium value is well-supported by HPB Singapore restaurant noodle soup benchmarks. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entries. Overall confidence is medium due to composite estimation methodology.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the tonkotsu ramen composite dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database; individual component fatty acid data could be summed but introduces compounding estimation error beyond acceptable precision. water_g set to -1: moisture content of the composite dish (including broth) is not reported as a single value in any approved source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace for this dish composition per USDA component data). GL per serving recalculated as (GI 55 × 72g available carbohydrate per 600g bowl) / 100 = 39.6; input record reported 37.4 which used a slightly different carbohydrate estimate. The dish is NOT halal due to pork bone broth and chashu pork belly. Tonkotsu Ramen originated in Kurume, Fukuoka Prefecture, Kyushu, Japan in the 1940s and was popularised in Hakata (Fukuoka city). Singapore restaurant versions served at Ippudo, Ramen Keisuke, Ichiran, Menya Musashi, Ramen Nagi, and Santouka. NOVA Classification: Class 3 (processed culinary preparation). Source note: Japan MEXT food composition database is not on the approved source list; nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore as required by Project Veritas source policy.
